conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your inquiry. I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit. To assess this, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have one child, Alex. He's 19 and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to understand his current situation.

Is Alex currently in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is. He's still in college doing his A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect. One final question: is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, yes actually. His other parent lives with us and they already claim the Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

While Alex meets all the criteria for Child Benefit (he's 19, in approved education doing his A-levels, and lives with you in the UK), the key issue is that **only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child**. Since Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for him, you cannot also claim it.

According to the rules, when two people are responsible for the same child, they need to agree between themselves who will claim. Two people cannot receive Child Benefit for the same child simultaneously.

If you wanted to be the one claiming Child Benefit for Alex instead, the current claimant (Alex's other parent) would need to contact the Child Benefit Office to stop their claim, and then you could make a new claim.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 19 years old and in approved education (doing A-levels at college), which means he meets the age criteria as children under 20 in approved education or training qualify. You live in the UK and Alex lives with you, which meets the residency and responsibility requirements. However, according to the Child Benefit rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child. Since Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for him, you are not eligible to claim it as well. The rules state that if two people are responsible for the same child, they must agree between themselves who will claim, and two people cannot get Child Benefit for the same child.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all the basic eligibility criteria - they live in the UK, are responsible for a 19-year-old child who is in approved education. However, they are not eligible to claim Child Benefit because Alex's other parent is already claiming it for him, and the rules clearly state that only one person can receive Child Benefit for any individual child.

You live in the UK and Alex lives with you, which meets the residency and responsibility requirements. However, according to the Child Benefit rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child. Since Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for him, you are not eligible to claim it as well. The rules state that if two people are responsible for the same child, they must agree between themselves who will claim, and two people cannot get Child Benefit for the same child.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all the basic eligibility criteria - they live in the UK, are responsible for a 19-year-old child who is in approved education. However, they are not eligible to claim Child Benefit because Alex's other parent is already claiming it for him, and the rules clearly state that only one person can receive Child Benefit for any individual child.
